title: IndiGo Doubles A350-900 Order to 60 in Long-Haul Push
dek: India's largest carrier converts an outstanding commitment into a firm deal, locking in 60 Airbus A350-900s and a Rolls-Royce Trent XWB engine package as it builds a widebody backbone for intercontinental flying.
body:
IndiGo, India's biggest airline by domestic market share, finalised a firm order for 30 additional Airbus A350-900s on 17 October 2025, converting an earlier memorandum of understanding and bringing its total commitment for the type to 60 aircraft. The carrier, historically an all-narrowbody low-cost operator, paired the airframe order with a deal covering Rolls-Royce Trent XWB engines to power the fleet. The move cements one of the largest widebody commitments ever placed by an Indian airline and signals a decisive shift toward long-haul flying.

From domestic champion to global ambitions

IndiGo has long dominated India's domestic skies with a fleet built almost entirely around the Airbus A320 family. The A350-900 order gives it a true intercontinental aircraft capable of serving Europe, North America and beyond from hubs such as Delhi, Mumbai and Bengaluru. Chief executive Pieter Elbers has framed the airline's current chapter as a transition toward becoming a major global carrier rather than a regional discount operator.

To bridge the gap before its own widebodies arrive, IndiGo has leaned on damp-leased Boeing 787-9s for early long-haul routes, adding European destinations to its map. Narrowbody Airbus A321XLRs are also expected to extend its reach on thinner long, thin routes.

Why the A350-900 fits

The A350-900 offers long range, twin-engine efficiency and a widebody cabin suited to premium and economy demand on competitive international corridors. Key attractions for a carrier scaling up include:

  • Range comfortably spanning India to most of Europe and parts of North America
  • Lower fuel burn per seat than older-generation widebodies
  • A cabin able to support a multi-class product as IndiGo moves upmarket

Delivery timing will determine how quickly the airline can deploy the type, but the enlarged order underscores expectations of sustained growth in Indian outbound travel. It also intensifies competition with a revamping Air India, setting up a two-horse race to define India's long-haul future.
